title: Sort a nearly sorted array (k-sorted)
description: Sort an array where each element is at most k positions away

examples.explanation:
Start: [6,5,3,2,8,10,9], k=3
i=1: Compare 5 with elements from index 0 to max(0,1-3)=0. 5<6, shift 6 right, insert 5 at index 0 → [5,6,3,2,8,10,9]
i=2: Compare 3 with elements from index 1 to max(0,2-3)=0. 3<6, shift 6 right; 3<5, shift 5 right; insert 3 at index 0 → [3,5,6,2,8,10,9]
i=3: Compare 2 with elements from index 2 to max(0,3-3)=0. 2<6, shift 6 right; 2<5, shift 5 right; 2<3, shift 3 right; insert 2 at index 0 → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=4: Compare 8 with elements from index 3 to max(0,4-3)=1. 8>6, no shift needed → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=5: Compare 10 with elements from index 4 to max(0,5-3)=2. 10>8, no shift needed → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=6: Compare 9 with elements from index 5 to max(0,6-3)=3. 9<10, shift 10 right; 9>8, insert 9 at index 5 → [2,3,5,6,8,9,10]
Final: [2,3,5,6,8,9,10]

Start: [10,9,8,7,4,70,60,50], k=4
i=1: Compare 9 with elements from index 0 to max(0,1-4)=0. 9<10, shift 10 right, insert 9 at index 0 → [9,10,8,7,4,70,60,50]
i=2: Compare 8 with elements from index 1 to max(0,2-4)=0. 8<10, shift 10 right; 8<9, shift 9 right; insert 8 at index 0 → [8,9,10,7,4,70,60,50]
i=3: Compare 7 with elements from index 2 to max(0,3-4)=0. 7<10, shift 10 right; 7<9, shift 9 right; 7<8, shift 8 right; insert 7 at index 0 → [7,8,9,10,4,70,60,50]
i=4: Compare 4 with elements from index 3 to max(0,4-4)=0. 4<10, shift 10 right; 4<9, shift 9 right; 4<8, shift 8 right; 4<7, shift 7 right; insert 4 at index 0 → [4,7,8,9,10,70,60,50]
i=5: Compare 70 with elements from index 4 to max(0,5-4)=1. 70>10, no shift needed → [4,7,8,9,10,70,60,50]
i=6: Compare 60 with elements from index 5 to max(0,6-4)=2. 60<70, shift 70 right; 60>10, insert 60 at index 5 → [4,7,8,9,10,60,70,50]
i=7: Compare 50 with elements from index 6 to max(0,7-4)=3. 50<70, shift 70 right; 50<60, shift 60 right; 50>10, insert 50 at index 5 → [4,7,8,9,10,50,60,70]
Final: [4,7,8,9,10,50,60,70]

approaches.name: Approach 2: Optimized Insertion Sort
approaches.summary: Use insertion sort but limit comparisons to at most k positions for each element, since each element is at most k away from its correct spot. This reduces the inner loop from O(n) to O(k), making it efficient for small k values.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort a Nearly Sorted Array Using Optimized Insertion Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of sorting an array where each element is at most k positions away from its correct sorted position using insertion sort with k-limited comparisons.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires sorting an array that is nearly sorted, meaning each element is at most k positions away from where it should be in the fully sorted array. The optimized insertion sort approach leverages this property by modifying the standard insertion sort algorithm. Instead of comparing each element with all previous elements (which would be O(n) comparisons per element), we only compare it with at most k previous elements. This works because if an element is at most k positions away from its correct spot, we only need to look k positions back to find where it belongs. The algorithm iterates through the array from index 1 to n-1. For each element at index i, it compares it with elements from i-1 down to max(0, i-k), shifting elements right as needed until the correct position is found. This reduces the inner loop from O(n) to O(k), making the overall time complexity O(nk). The space complexity remains O(1) as it sorts in-place. This approach is particularly efficient when k is small compared to n. Common mistakes include not properly handling the boundary condition when i-k is negative, which can cause index errors. Another mistake is forgetting to shift elements correctly during the insertion process. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the key subroutine of inserting an element into a nearly sorted segment with k-limited comparisons.

examples.trace:
Start: [6,5,3,2,8,10,9], k=3
i=1: Compare 5 with elements from index 0 to max(0,1-3)=0. 5<6, shift 6 right, insert 5 at index 0 → [5,6,3,2,8,10,9]
i=2: Compare 3 with elements from index 1 to max(0,2-3)=0. 3<6, shift 6 right; 3<5, shift 5 right; insert 3 at index 0 → [3,5,6,2,8,10,9]
i=3: Compare 2 with elements from index 2 to max(0,3-3)=0. 2<6, shift 6 right; 2<5, shift 5 right; 2<3, shift 3 right; insert 2 at index 0 → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=4: Compare 8 with elements from index 3 to max(0,4-3)=1. 8>6, no shift needed → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=5: Compare 10 with elements from index 4 to max(0,5-3)=2. 10>8, no shift needed → [2,3,5,6,8,10,9]
i=6: Compare 9 with elements from index 5 to max(0,6-3)=3. 9<10, shift 10 right; 9>8, insert 9 at index 5 → [2,3,5,6,8,9,10]
Final: [2,3,5,6,8,9,10]

Start: [10,9,8,7,4,70,60,50], k=4
i=1: Compare 9 with elements from index 0 to max(0,1-4)=0. 9<10, shift 10 right, insert 9 at index 0 → [9,10,8,7,4,70,60,50]
i=2: Compare 8 with elements from index 1 to max(0,2-4)=0. 8<10, shift 10 right; 8<9, shift 9 right; insert 8 at index 0 → [8,9,10,7,4,70,60,50]
i=3: Compare 7 with elements from index 2 to max(0,3-4)=0. 7<10, shift 10 right; 7<9, shift 9 right; 7<8, shift 8 right; insert 7 at index 0 → [7,8,9,10,4,70,60,50]
i=4: Compare 4 with elements from index 3 to max(0,4-4)=0. 4<10, shift 10 right; 4<9, shift 9 right; 4<8, shift 8 right; 4<7, shift 7 right; insert 4 at index 0 → [4,7,8,9,10,70,60,50]
i=5: Compare 70 with elements from index 4 to max(0,5-4)=1. 70>10, no shift needed → [4,7,8,9,10,70,60,50]
i=6: Compare 60 with elements from index 5 to max(0,6-4)=2. 60<70, shift 70 right; 60>10, insert 60 at index 5 → [4,7,8,9,10,60,70,50]
i=7: Compare 50 with elements from index 6 to max(0,7-4)=3. 50<70, shift 70 right; 50<60, shift 60 right; 50>10, insert 50 at index 5 → [4,7,8,9,10,50,60,70]
Final: [4,7,8,9,10,50,60,70]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Element into Nearly Sorted Segment with K-Limited Comparisons
ladders.desc: Implement the core insertion step that places one element into its correct position by comparing with at most k previous elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the optimized insertion sort for nearly sorted arrays. Given an array, a current index i, and a value k, the task is to insert the element at index i into the correct position within the segment from index max(0, i-k) to i-1. The intuition is that since the element is at most k positions away from its sorted position, we only need to look back k elements to find where it belongs. The algorithm works by storing the current element in a temporary variable. Then, starting from index i-1 and moving leftwards to max(0, i-k), we compare the temporary element with each element. If the temporary element is smaller, we shift that element one position to the right. We continue this process until we find an element that is smaller than or equal to the temporary element, or until we reach the left boundary. Finally, we place the temporary element at the correct position. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(k) since we perform at most k comparisons and shifts. Space complexity is O(1) as we use only a constant amount of extra space. This sub-routine feeds into the full problem (L0) by being called for each element in the array. Common mistakes include not correctly handling the boundary when i-k is negative, which can lead to index errors. Another mistake is forgetting to shift elements properly, which can overwrite data. The real code pattern involves a while loop that moves from i-1 downwards, with careful index management.

examples.trace:
Start: arr=[2,3,5,6,8,10,9], i=6, k=3
Store temp = arr[6] = 9
j = i-1 = 5
Compare temp (9) with arr[5]=10: 9<10 → shift arr[5] right to index 6 → arr=[2,3,5,6,8,10,10]
j = 4
Compare temp (9) with arr[4]=8: 9>8 → stop shifting
Insert temp at index j+1 = 5 → arr[5]=9 → arr=[2,3,5,6,8,9,10]
Final: [2,3,5,6,8,9,10]

Start: arr=[4,7,8,9,10,70,60,50], i=7, k=4
Store temp = arr[7] = 50
j = i-1 = 6
Compare temp (50) with arr[6]=70: 50<70 → shift arr[6] right to index 7 → arr=[4,7,8,9,10,70,60,70]
j = 5
Compare temp (50) with arr[5]=60: 50<60 → shift arr[5] right to index 6 → arr=[4,7,8,9,10,70,60,70] (note: arr[6] was 60, now 60 from shift)
j = 4
Compare temp (50) with arr[4]=10: 50>10 → stop shifting
Insert temp at index j+1 = 5 → arr[5]=50 → arr=[4,7,8,9,10,50,60,70]
Final: [4,7,8,9,10,50,60,70]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Leveraging K-Limited Comparisons in Insertion Sort
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the insight that in a nearly sorted array, each element needs at most k comparisons to find its correct position.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the optimized insertion sort for nearly sorted arrays is based on the property that each element is at most k positions away from its correct sorted position. This means that when inserting an element into the sorted portion of the array, we don't need to compare it with all previous elements—only with at most k elements to its left. The intuition is that if an element is displaced by at most k positions, its correct position must lie within k indices to the left in the sorted order. Therefore, by limiting comparisons to k previous elements, we reduce the inner loop of insertion sort from O(n) to O(k). The algorithm involves iterating through the array and for each element, comparing it with up to k previous elements and shifting them right if necessary until the correct spot is found. This logic is efficient when k is small, making it O(nk) overall. The space complexity remains O(1). This core logic feeds into L1 by providing the reasoning behind why the key sub-routine only needs to look back k elements. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the k property and comparing with more than k elements, which defeats the optimization. Another mistake is not adjusting the comparison range correctly when near the start of the array. The real code pattern involves a loop that goes from 1 to n-1, and within it, a while loop that moves leftwards up to k steps.

examples.output: Concept: For each element, compare with at most 3 previous elements.
examples.trace:
Element at index 0 (6): No previous elements to compare.
Element at index 1 (5): Compare with index 0 (6) only (since max(0,1-3)=0). 5<6, so shift and insert.
Element at index 2 (3): Compare with indices 1 and 0 (since max(0,2-3)=0). 3<5 and 3<6, shift both and insert.
Element at index 3 (2): Compare with indices 2,1,0 (since max(0,3-3)=0). 2<3,2<5,2<6, shift all and insert.
Element at index 4 (8): Compare with indices 3,2,1 (since max(0,4-3)=1). 8>2,8>3,8>5, so no shift needed.
Thus, each element compared with at most k=3 previous elements.

examples.output: Concept: For each element, compare with at most 4 previous elements.
examples.trace:
Element at index 0 (10): No previous elements.
Element at index 1 (9): Compare with index 0 (10) only (max(0,1-4)=0). 9<10, shift and insert.
Element at index 2 (8): Compare with indices 1 and 0 (max(0,2-4)=0). 8<9 and 8<10, shift both and insert.
Element at index 3 (7): Compare with indices 2,1,0 (max(0,3-4)=0). 7<8,7<9,7<10, shift all and insert.
Element at index 4 (4): Compare with indices 3,2,1,0 (max(0,4-4)=0). 4<7,4<8,4<9,4<10, shift all and insert.
Thus, each element compared with at most k=4 previous elements.

testCases.expected: Each element compared with at most 1 previous element.
Element at index 1 compared with index 0; element at index 2 compared with index 1.
Element at index 2 compared with indices 1 and 0; element at index 3 compared with indices 2 and 1.
Element at index 3 compared with indices 2 and 1.
Element at index 3 compared with indices 2 and 1; element at index 4 compared with indices 3 and 2.
No comparisons needed for any element beyond index 0.
Each element compared with up to 4 previous elements.
Element at index 2 compared with indices 1 and 0; element at index 3 compared with indices 2,1,0.
Each element compared with at most 1 previous element.

ladders.title: Building Block — Shifting Elements in an Array During Insertion
ladders.desc: Implement the operation of shifting elements to the right to make space for inserting a new element in a sorted segment.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental operation of shifting elements within an array to create space for inserting a new element. In insertion sort, when we find the correct position for an element, we need to move all larger elements one position to the right to accommodate it. The intuition is that arrays in memory are contiguous, so inserting an element requires shifting subsequent elements. The algorithm involves starting from the end of the segment and moving each element one index to the right until we reach the insertion point. For example, if we want to insert a value at index j, we shift elements from index j to i-1 (where i is the current position) rightwards. This is done in a loop that decrements an index variable, copying each element to the next position. The time complexity for shifting m elements is O(m), and space complexity is O(1) as it operates in-place. This building block feeds into L2 by being a critical step in the k-limited comparison logic—once we determine where to insert, we shift elements to make room. Common mistakes include shifting in the wrong direction (e.g., left instead of right) or overwriting elements incorrectly. Another mistake is not stopping the shift at the correct index. The real code pattern uses a while loop that moves from i-1 down to j, assigning arr[pos+1] = arr[pos].

examples.input: arr = [2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 10, 10], shift from index 5 to make space at index 5
examples.trace: Start: arr=[2,3,5,6,8,10,10], need to shift elements from index 5 to index 5 (i.e., no shift if same, but typically we shift from higher indices). Example: To insert at index 5, shift from index 5 to 5: arr[6] is already 10, so no change. But for clarity, if shifting from index 4 to make space at index 3: Start arr=[2,3,5,6,8,10,9], shift index 4 to 5: arr[5]=arr[4]=8 → arr=[2,3,5,6,8,8,9]; shift index 3 to 4: arr[4]=arr[3]=6 → arr=[2,3,5,6,6,8,9]; now space at index 3 for insertion.

examples.input: arr = [4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 70, 60, 70], shift from index 5 to make space at index 5
examples.trace: Start: arr=[4,7,8,9,10,70,60,70], need to shift to insert at index 5. Shift from index 6 to 7: arr[7]=arr[6]=60 → arr=[4,7,8,9,10,70,60,60]; shift from index 5 to 6: arr[6]=arr[5]=70 → arr=[4,7,8,9,10,70,70,60]; now space at index 5 for insertion.

testCases.input: arr = [1, 3, 3], shift from index 1 to make space at index 1
arr = [2, 4, 4, 6, 5], shift from index 2 to make space at index 2
arr = [3, 5, 6, 2, 8], shift from index 3 to make space at index 2
arr = [8, 9, 10, 7, 4], shift from index 3 to make space at index 2
arr = [1, 2, 4, 5, 3], shift from index 4 to make space at index 3
arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], shift from index 4 to make space at index 4
arr = [4, 5, 3, 2, 1], shift from index 2 to make space at index 1
arr = [1], shift from index 0 to make space at index 0
arr = [2, 3, 6, 12, 56], shift from index 3 to make space at index 2
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50], shift from index 4 to make space at index 4

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Swapping Adjacent Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Perform the fundamental operation of comparing two adjacent elements in an array and swapping them if they are out of order.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is a cornerstone of many sorting algorithms, including insertion sort. It involves comparing two elements that are next to each other in an array and swapping their positions if the first element is greater than the second, to move towards a sorted order. The intuition is that sorting often requires rearranging elements based on comparisons, and adjacent comparisons are simple and efficient for small segments. The algorithm checks if arr[i] > arr[i+1] for some index i. If true, it swaps the elements using a temporary variable: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[i+1]; arr[i+1] = temp. This operation has a time complexity of O(1) per comparison and swap, and space complexity of O(1) for the temporary variable. It feeds into L3 as shifting elements in insertion sort can be thought of as repeated swaps or direct assignments, but understanding comparison and swapping is foundational. Common mistakes include forgetting to use a temporary variable during the swap, which can lead to data loss. Another mistake is comparing non-adjacent elements when the task specifies adjacent ones. The real code pattern is a simple if-statement followed by a three-line swap.

examples.input: arr = [5, 3], compare and swap if needed
examples.trace:
Start: arr=[5,3]
Compare arr[0]=5 and arr[1]=3: 5>3, so swap.
Swap: temp=5, arr[0]=3, arr[1]=5
Final: [3,5]

examples.input: arr = [2, 4, 3], compare arr[1] and arr[2], swap if needed
examples.trace:
Start: arr=[2,4,3]
Compare arr[1]=4 and arr[2]=3: 4>3, so swap.
Swap: temp=4, arr[1]=3, arr[2]=4
Final: [2,3,4]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of arrays, including how to access elements using indices and iterate through an array.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers arrays, which are fundamental data structures in programming. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient access to elements based on their position. Key operations include accessing an element at a specific index (e.g., arr[i]), updating an element (arr[i] = value), and iterating through all elements using a loop. For example, to traverse an array of size n, we can use a for-loop from i=0 to n-1. Understanding zero-based indexing is crucial: the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. This concept feeds into L4 as comparing and swapping elements requires accessing array elements by their indices. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing by accident or accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., arr[n] for an array of size n). Another mistake is confusing the array size with the last index. The real code pattern involves declaring an array and using loops with index variables.

examples.trace:
Array arr has elements: index 0: 10, index 1: 20, index 2: 30.
Access arr[1] returns the value at index 1, which is 20.

examples.input: arr = [5, 3, 8, 1], iterate and print each element
examples.trace:
Array arr has size 4.
Loop i from 0 to 3:
i=0: print arr[0]=5
i=1: print arr[1]=3
i=2: print arr[2]=8
i=3: print arr[3]=1
Output: 5, 3, 8, 1
